creative_brief: Split-screen hero: left side shows a supermarket meat tray with a generic green label reading only 'Product of Australia'; right side shows the same cut with a clear handwritten-style tag listing farm name, location, practice, and distance. Muted, honest tones — no glossy food styling. The contrast should feel immediate and uncomfortable.
headline: Do you actually know what farm your chicken came from?
subhead: Not the brand. Not the logo. The actual farm — the soil it was raised on, the practice used, the kilometres it travelled. If you can't answer that, the system is working exactly as designed.
hero_copy: Supermarkets spend millions on packaging that feels trustworthy. Green labels. Rolling-hills imagery. Words like "free range" and "natural." But ask one simple question — which farm? — and the trail goes cold. That silence isn't an accident. It's a feature. FarmThru is a farm-direct grocery store that names every farm, every practice, every kilometre. Because transparency isn't a marketing word to us. It's the entire point.

sections.heading: The silence is the strategy
sections.body:
A conventional supply chain passes your food through four or five middlemen before it reaches a shelf. Each handoff strips away a piece of information. By the time you're reading the label, all that's left is "Product of Australia" — a fact so broad it tells you almost nothing.

You can't verify the farming practice. You can't trace the distance. You don't know if the farmer was paid fairly or squeezed to breaking point. You're trusting a system that profits from you not asking questions.

FarmThru works differently. We partner with roughly 15–20 farms and suppliers across NSW. You order online, collect from our Brookvale hub Monday to Friday. Every product traces to a named farm, a named method, a real distance — straight from farm to hub. No unnecessary middlemen standing between you and the truth about your food.

sections.heading: What transparency actually looks like
sections.body:
It's not a QR code that leads to a corporate "sustainability" page. It's this: you pick up your order from Brookvale and you can see which farm raised that lamb, how far it travelled, and what practice the farmer used. No guessing. No clever label design doing the work of actual information.

There's no subscription. No lock-in. No membership fee. You order when you want, collect when it suits you. Buy in bulk and you save more — rewarding commitment, not trapping you in one.

This is a grocery store built around one radical idea: you deserve to know what you're feeding your family. Every single time.

sections.heading: Own a piece of the store that has nothing to hide
sections.body:
We're opening FarmThru to equity crowdfunding through Birchal — so the people who care about food transparency can actually own a piece of the solution.

A $5 refundable deposit gets you VIP access: first look at the campaign, priority updates, and exclusive Q&A with the team. No commitment. Just a front-row seat.

Investment starts at $50. The maximum is $10,000 per investor. This is a chance to be part of a grocery store that pays farmers first and shows customers everything — not just what looks good on a label.

Investing in early-stage businesses carries real risk, including the potential loss of your entire investment. This isn't a guaranteed return. It's a bet on a better food system — eyes wide open.

sections.heading: Compliance
sections.body: FarmThru's equity crowdfunding campaign is hosted on Birchal. Investing in early-stage companies is speculative and carries high risk, including the risk of loss of your entire investment, illiquidity, and dilution. Returns are not guaranteed. This content is not financial advice. You should consider whether this investment is appropriate for your circumstances and read the offer document in full before investing.
